title: Pet Urine on Wool Carpet — Cold + Urease Enzyme (Wool-Safe)
stainChemistry: Pet urine contains urea, uric acid, urobilin pigment, and bacterial byproducts. On wool carpet the uric acid crystallizes into the alpha-keratin scales and binds tightly as it dries. Warm water swells wool fiber and opens scales, which lets standard protein-digesting enzymes (protease) penetrate and permanently damage the keratin. A urease-only enzymatic cleaner breaks down urea into ammonia and CO2 without attacking the wool itself, which is why it is the only enzyme class considered wool-safe. Wool carpet adds a second failure mode: over-wetting saturates the jute or latex backing and causes shrinkage, brown-out wicking, and mold.
whyThisWorks: Cold water with a urease-based enzymatic / bio-enzymatic cleaner addresses the odor chemistry without breaking the wool. Gentle blotting and damp (not wet) application protects the carpet backing. Pile-direction handling prevents matting of the tufts. This protocol is deliberately conservative — for deeply saturated, aged, or backing-penetrating pet urine the correct answer is a professional wool rug specialist, not a more aggressive chemistry.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White terry towel, cold water, weighted object
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot up as much pet urine as possible with a dry white towel. Press — do not rub — to avoid distorting the wool pile. Once the towel stops picking up moisture, lightly mist the area with cold water and continue blotting. Keep the carpet damp, never soaking — over-wetting the backing is the main cause of wool rug damage.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Urease-only enzymatic pet cleaner, cold water, white cloth
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 30-60 minutes (extend to 4-6 hours for aged stains)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a urease-based bio-enzymatic pet cleaner diluted in COLD water per product instructions. Urease is the wool-safe enzyme class — it breaks down urea and neutralizes odor without digesting wool keratin. Standard protease-based enzyme cleaners will permanently damage wool and MUST NOT be used. Keep the area damp, covered with a clean white towel, and allow the enzyme to work. For aged or crystallized pet urine, extend the dwell time rather than increasing concentration or temperature.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Nature's Miracle (cold water application only), Rocco & Roxie Professional Strength, Anti Icky Poo — all urease-based, all applied cold

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lightly mist the treated area with cold water and blot repeatedly with clean white towels until the towels come away clean. Do not flood the carpet. The goal is to lift the dissolved urine and enzyme residue without driving water into the backing.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Blot immediately with dry white towels, then cold water rinse and blot again. No enzyme needed if caught within minutes.
WITHIN HOURS: Cold water blot, then urease-based bio-enzymatic cleaner dwell 30-60 minutes, cold rinse, air dry elevated.
AGED / DRIED: Extended urease dwell (4-6 hours) in cold water. If odor persists after two treatments, or if the urine has clearly penetrated the backing, stop and call a wool rug specialist.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water or warm water — swells wool fiber, opens keratin scales, and allows enzyme penetration into the protein
NEVER use protease-based enzyme cleaners — protease digests wool keratin and causes permanent fiber damage
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys wool keratin on contact
NEVER use hydrogen peroxide on untested wool — can yellow or bleach the fiber
NEVER use ammonia — already present in the urine chemistry and further alkali attacks wool
NEVER over-wet the carpet — backing saturation causes shrinkage, brown-out wicking, and mold
NEVER rub or scrub — distorts the pile and breaks wet wool fibers

neverDo: Apply protease enzyme cleaner to wool carpet
Use hot water on wool rugs at any stage
Apply chlorine bleach or peroxide to wool carpet
Flood the carpet — keep moisture shallow to protect backing
Rub or agitate wet wool pile

escalation: If pet urine has soaked into the backing, if odor persists after two full urease treatments, if the rug is antique, hand-knotted, or silk-blend, or if brown wicking appears during drying — stop home treatment. A professional wool rug specialist with an in-plant bath and controlled-drying room is the safe path. DIY escalation on a compromised wool rug causes more damage than the original stain.

household: Urease-based bio-enzymatic pet cleaner (cold water only)
professional: In-plant wool rug wash with pH-neutral shampoo
Post-treatment wool conditioner and pile-reset grooming

professionalProtocol.reason: Wool rugs with pet urine saturation are the single most common wool-rug damage scenario. A specialist shop with immersion washing and a drying room is dramatically safer than any topical home treatment once the urine reaches the backing.
professionalProtocol.findPro: Search for 'wool rug cleaner' or 'oriental rug specialist' in your area — not a general carpet cleaner.

aftercare: Blot the area as dry as possible. If the rug is small enough to lift, raise the affected area off the floor with blocks or upturned glasses so air can reach both faces — this prevents brown-out wicking and backing mold. Use a fan on low, never heat. Do not vacuum until fully dry. Brush the pile back into direction once the fiber is barely damp.
